text: this is a demonstration of robotic low anti resection this is the patient details these are the port positions we use six ports this port position is meant for splenic flexural mobilization
After completing splenic flexure mobilization, the surgeon begins the first part of the surgery, approaching from medial to lateral using robotic instruments.
The surgeon clears the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) of fibrolymphatic structures, revealing the vessel's clean surface.
The surgical procedure continues with dissection behind the mesocolon, where the ureter is clearly visible.

The pelvic nerves are visible as the surgeon secures and transects the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The Gerota's fascia is pushed downward, revealing the pancreas as the dissection progresses upward.
The surgeon identifies a bulge of the pancreas, with the transverse mesocolon running over it. The transverse mesocolon is transected, and the inferior mesenteric vein is secured.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure by dissecting the gastrocolic omentum and freeing the splenic flexure from surrounding attachments.
At this stage we hop the robotic arm two and three.
The surgeon continues the pelvic dissection, working carefully around the visible anatomical structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, pelvic nerve, and gonadal vessels, while performing mesorectal dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs an anterior dissection of the rectum, carefully separating the rectal tissue from surrounding structures.
This patient has undergone abdominal hysterectomy in her younger days.
The surgeon encounters severe adhesions in the anterior aspect of the surgical field during the dissection process.
The surgeon performs dissection on the posterior aspect of the rectum, continuing the pelvic dissection process.
The pelvic floor is clearly visualized, showing the muscular structure at the base of the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon continues the anterior dissection of the rectum, navigating through the tissue planes with visible adhesions from a previous abdominal hysterectomy.
The surgeon clears fibro fatty tissue from the posterior wall of the vagina, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the glistening white layer as the vaginal wall during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pelvic floor, maintaining a clear view of the anatomical structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon demonstrates the completely mobilized rectum, which is transected using a green stapler. An anastomosis is then performed between the rectal stump and the colon.
